behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure is at a module boundary: raw-body extraction + signature verification + enqueue handoff. A pure unit test of the verifier can pass while handler wiring still forwards invalid payloads.
behaviors.test_description: Run the webhook route with real request parsing middleware and a real handler instance, send a signed request with a tampered body, assert HTTP 401 and assert queue storage remains unchanged (no enqueue record).

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across handler and persistence modules. Unit tests with mocked queue can miss real commit/await ordering bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Use the real queue implementation against local persistent storage (e.g., sqlite/file-backed queue). Issue a valid webhook and assert: by the time 200 is observed, the event is already durably present in queue storage.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: The failure appears at interaction boundary between request handler and async worker trigger. Unit logic checks can miss accidental await/blocking in wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Configure downstream processor to sleep/block for a long interval, send a valid webhook, assert response is returned quickly while processor is still in-progress, then assert processor completion later.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a runtime latency/concurrency SLO. Real timing under concurrent load can be hidden by mocks and in-process shortcuts; needs real server/runtime behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service in a real process, create concurrent load with earlier events causing slow downstream work, send a fresh valid webhook, and measure end-to-end wall-clock response time at client side; assert p95 or worst-case response under test condition is <3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness depends on handler-to-persistent-queue interaction (idempotency key/unique constraint). Unit tests can miss duplicate writes caused by storage semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Using real persistent queue storage, send the same event ID twice (including near-simultaneous sends), assert first is accepted, second is not enqueued, and only one durable record exists.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is at boundary between queue capacity signal and HTTP mapping. Mock-only unit tests can miss incorrect translation of real queue error/state to response.
behaviors.test_description: Fill the real queue to configured limit, send another valid webhook, assert HTTP 503 and presence/value format of Retry-After, and confirm no additional queue write occurred.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This targets the queue component with real persistence semantics (transactions/constraints). Unit tests against in-memory mocks can hide true overflow behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate queue with a small real configured capacity in local persistent storage, enqueue up to limit, attempt one more, assert explicit rejection/error and verify durable item count never exceeds configured cap.
